title: Peanut brittle
subtitle: Thin, shatteringly crisp toffee embedded with roasted peanuts
excerpt: One large sheet of peanut brittle: caster sugar, golden syrup, and butter cooked to 154°C (hard crack stage), then soda is stirred in to aerate the toffee and make it less dense, poured over roasted peanuts on a buttered tray, and left to set in a thin sheet. Breaks into shards.
sourceNotes: Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136: the nut toffee section documents a hard crack sugar confection (cooked until a thread of syrup dropped into cold water snaps cleanly) poured over roasted nuts. The use of bicarbonate of soda to aerate the finished toffee and reduce its density is documented in British domestic confectionery from the late Victorian period. Peanut brittle using roasted peanuts in hard-crack toffee is documented across domestic confectionery literature of the period; the recipe here follows the public-domain method.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Keeps in an airtight tin, separated by baking paper, for 2 weeks in a dry environment. Humidity makes it sticky.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: target: hard crack stage (154°C) — use a sugar thermometer

recipeTools.notes: Large, lipped baking tray, well greased or lined with a silicone mat.

glossaryTerms.definition: The point in sugar cooking when the syrup reaches 149–155°C. At this temperature, almost all water has been driven off and the sugar is purely liquid. To test without a thermometer: drop a small amount of syrup into a cup of cold water — it will set immediately into rigid, glass-like threads that snap cleanly when bent. Below hard crack, the brittle will not shatter cleanly; above it, the sugar begins to caramelise and burn. Move quickly: the temperature continues to rise after you remove the pan from the heat.

content.text: Brittle is one of the simplest sugar confections: cook to a high temperature, aerate with bicarbonate, pour over nuts, and leave to set. The critical point is the
at 154°C. At this temperature, the sugar sets into glass on the tray. At lower temperatures, it sets tacky and sticks to teeth. A sugar thermometer is the only reliable way to hit this.

attrs.title: Hot sugar is extremely dangerous
attrs.body: Sugar syrup at 154°C causes severe burns on contact with skin and sticks. Keep children away from the hob. Pour the brittle carefully and do not touch or taste the mixture until it is completely cold and solid. If any syrup contacts skin, run immediately under cold water for at least 10 minutes.

items.prepNote: roasted and salted — the salt works with the sweet toffee
to scatter over the hot brittle

content.text: Grease a large lipped baking tray generously with butter or line with a silicone mat. Scatter the {{peanuts}} roasted peanuts over the tray in an even layer. Have the {{bicarbonate-of-soda}} bicarbonate of soda measured and ready in a small bowl beside the hob — the soda must go in the moment the toffee comes off the heat.

Combine the {{caster-sugar}}, {{golden-syrup}}, and {{water}} in a heavy-based saucepan over medium heat, stirring until the sugar dissolves. Stop stirring once dissolved and bring to a boil. Add the {{unsalted-butter}} and cook without stirring until the thermometer reads 154°C, about 12–15 minutes. Watch closely from 145°C: the temperature rises fast at this stage.

The moment the toffee reaches 154°C, remove from the heat and immediately stir in the {{bicarbonate-of-soda}} (it will foam and bubble). Stir briefly to combine and pour immediately over the peanuts on the prepared tray. Scatter {{sea-salt-flakes}} sea salt flakes over the top if using. Do not spread with a spoon — let the toffee self-level. Leave to cool and harden completely at room temperature for 30 minutes. Break into shards by hand or with the back of a spoon.
